steps.title: Identify the structure around the blank
steps.content: After the blank, the sentence has "assume that people are consistently rational decision-makers—cannot explain." That trailing em dash closes a nonessential interrupter. The interrupter is the relative clause beginning with 'which' that describes neoclassical economic models.

steps.title: Apply the matching-punctuation rule
steps.content: A nonessential clause set off mid-sentence must be bracketed by matching marks on both sides — em dash with em dash, or comma with comma — never mixed. Since the closing mark is an em dash, the opening mark before 'which' must also be an em dash.

steps.title: Eliminate the other choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'models — which': em dash on both sides of the interrupter — matching pair. ✓
  • Choice B — 'models, which': opens with a comma but closes with an em dash — mismatched bracketing punctuation. ✗
  • Choice C — 'models which': no opening punctuation at all, leaving the nonessential clause unbracketed on the left. ✗
  • Choice D — 'models which —': the dash falls inside the clause rather than before 'which,' so the clause still has no opening boundary on the left of 'which.' ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
